---
     1|# Decision Trees: Handling Edge Cases
     2|
     3|This guide helps you route to the right approach when common writing scenarios arise.
     4|
     5|## When Writer Says: "I'm Stuck, Don't Know How to Continue"
     6|
     7|**Diagnosis Flow**:
     8|
     9|```
    10|Ask: "What part of the process feels blocked?"
    11|
    12|├─ "I don't have an outline"
    13|│  └─ ROUTE: Back to Collaborative Outlining
    14|│     Questions: Topic clear? Audience clear? Structure makes sense?
    15|│
    16|├─ "Outline exists but I'm frozen on a section"
    17|│  └─ ROUTE: Section-Specific Unblocking
    18|│     Questions: Have you written the first draft? What's the section's main point?
    19|│
    20|├─ "I've written everything but it doesn't fit together"
    21|│  └─ ROUTE: Structure & Flow Review
    22|│     Actions: Review section transitions, check logical progression, verify outline still applies
    23|│
    24|└─ "I have research but can't explain it coherently"
    25|   └─ ROUTE: Research Synthesis Help
    26|      Actions: Help distill key points, identify 1-2 main claims, structure evidence
    27|```
    28|
    29|**What NOT to Do**: Rewrite the stuck section. Instead, ask diagnostic questions until the writer articulates what's missing.
    30|
    31|---
    32|
    33|## When Multiple Research Sources Contradict Each Other
    34|
    35|**Evaluation Matrix**:
    36|
    37|| Scenario | Example | Decision Rule |
    38|| --- | --- | --- |
    39|| **Same claim, different statistics** | "40% of teams use AI" vs "67% use AI" | Go with most recent, most reputable source. Cite the one you use. Note the disagreement if both are credible. |
    40|| **Different interpretation of same data** | Study shows X, but interpretations differ | Quote the actual data, let writer choose interpretation. Don't assume which is "right." |
    41|| **One source is much newer** | 2019 finding vs 2024 finding on same topic | Prefer recent unless older source is more authoritative or still accurate. Ask writer: "Should we use updated data?" |
    42|| **Sources have clear bias** | Company study vs independent research on same topic | Disclose bias in citation. Use independent source as primary, note company perspective as counterpoint. |
    43|| **Conflict in expert opinion** | Expert A says X, Expert B says Y | Both are valid. Include both perspectives if central to thesis, or ask writer which aligns with their argument. |
    44|
    45|**Protocol**:
    46|
    47|1. **Don't choose for the writer** — present options with source reputation
    48|2. **Explain the conflict** — "Both are credible, but they emphasize different aspects"
    49|3. **Ask for guidance** — "Which interpretation aligns with your thesis?"
    50|4. **Document the decision** — note why this source was chosen
    51|
    52|---
    53|
    54|## When Writer Rejects Your Feedback
    55|
    56|**Response Framework**:
    57|
    58|```
    59|Step 1: Listen (Don't Defend)
    60|├─ Ask: "What doesn't feel right about the feedback?"
    61|├─ Listen without rebuttal
    62|└─ Understand their reasoning
    63|
    64|Step 2: Validate (Even If You Disagree)
    65|├─ "I understand why that doesn't work for you"
    66|├─ Acknowledge their ownership
    67|└─ "It's your voice, your choice"
    68|
    69|Step 3: Offer Alternatives
    70|├─ "Here are two other approaches to the same problem..."
    71|├─ OR "Maybe that wasn't the right suggestion; what would help?"
    72|└─ Never push a single solution
    73|
    74|Step 4: Move Forward
    75|├─ Accept their choice gracefully
    76|├─ Don't revisit rejected feedback in next section
    77|└─ Adjust your feedback style based on what worked
    78|```
    79|
    80|**Key Principle**: Writer's voice > Your suggestion. Always.
    81|
    82|---
    83|
    84|## When Writer Wants Feedback on Technical vs Narrative Writing
    85|
    86|**Content-Type Routing**:
    87|
    88|| Content Type | Feedback Focus | What to Check | What to Skip |
    89|| --- | --- | --- | --- |
    90|| **Technical Documentation** | Clarity, accuracy, completeness | Are steps testable? Is jargon explained? Are edge cases noted? | Emotional hooks, narrative flow, personal voice |
    91|| **Blog Post** | Voice, engagement, pacing | Does hook grab? Are examples relatable? Does tone stay consistent? | Citation formality, exhaustive sources, academic rigor |
    92|| **Research Paper** | Argument strength, evidence, rigor | Are claims supported? Citations complete? Logic sound? | Narrative engagement, conversational tone, personality |
    93|| **Thought Leadership** | Unique POV, credibility, impact | Is the perspective original? Is it well-reasoned? Does it challenge assumptions? | Exhaustive research, neutral tone, balanced coverage |
    94|| **Case Study** | Narrative + data balance | Does story come alive? Are metrics clear? Is the transformation visible? | Academic rigor, exhaustive background, unrelated context |
    95|| **Newsletter** | Brevity, personality, relevance | Is it skimmable? Does voice come through? Is the takeaway clear? | Depth, comprehensive research, detailed citations |
    96|
    97|**Before Feedback Ask**: "What type of writing is this? What's your primary goal?"
    98|
    99|---
   100|
   101|## When Writer Asks: "Should I Include This?"
   102|
   103|**Inclusion Decision Tree**:
   104|
   105|```
   106|Question: "Does this serve the main argument?"
   107|
   108|├─ YES: "Keep it"
   109|│  └─ Does it support the reader's journey?
   110|│     ├─ YES → Position it strategically
   111|│     └─ NO → Move it to appendix or cut
   112|│
   113|└─ NO: "Consider cutting it"
   114|   ├─ Is it interesting but tangential?
   115|   │  ├─ YES → Offer two options:
   116|   │  │  1. Cut and link to separate piece
   117|   │  │  2. Create a sidebar/callout (doesn't interrupt flow)
   118|   │  └─ NO → Cut
   119|   │
   120|   └─ Is it a lost love (writer wants it despite weak relevance)?
   121|      ├─ YES → Compromise: one sentence reference only
   122|      └─ NO → Cut cleanly and move on
   123|```
   124|
   125|**Your Role**: Don't decide. Help writer decide by asking: "Does this move your main argument forward?"
   126|
   127|---
   128|
   129|## When Citations Need Consistency Audit
   130|
   131|**Citation Format Decision**:
   132|
   133|```
   134|Ask writer: "Which citation style do you prefer?"
   135|
   136|├─ INLINE: Author (Year) or [link]
   137|│  └─ Best for: Flowing narrative, web-based content, blogs
   138|│
   139|├─ NUMBERED: Superscript[1] with footnotes at end
   140|│  └─ Best for: Academic papers, long-form articles, complex citations
   141|│
   142|└─ FOOTNOTE: Endnote style with full citations
   143|   └─ Best for: Technical docs, formal reports, scholarly work
   144|```
   145|
   146|**Then**:
   147|
   148|- Audit entire document for consistency
   149|- Convert any mismatched citations to chosen style
   150|- Create a references list using that style
   151|- Flag any citations missing publication details
   152|
   153|**Red Flag**: If different sections use different styles, that's a professionalism issue. Fix it.
   154|
   155|---
   156|
   157|## When Writer Is Overwhelmed by Feedback
   158|
   159|**Triage & Prioritize**:
   160|
   161|```
   162|Identify: What type of feedback is most important?
   163|
   164|1. CRITICAL (must fix):
   165|   ├─ Factual errors or unsourced claims
   166|   ├─ Logical gaps in argument
   167|   └─ Missing citations that you flagged
   168|
   169|2. IMPORTANT (should improve):
   170|   ├─ Clarity issues affecting understanding
   171|   ├─ Flow/structure problems
   172|   └─ Weak transitions
   173|
   174|3. OPTIONAL (nice to have):
   175|   ├─ Word choice alternatives
   176|   ├─ Style tweaks
   177|   └─ Nice-to-have examples
   178|```
   179|
   180|**Then**: Present only CRITICAL + 2-3 IMPORTANT items. Save OPTIONAL for next pass.
   181|
   182|**Say**: "Here are the 3 things that will have biggest impact. Ready to tackle these?"
   183|
   184|---
   185|
   186|## When Writer Needs to Adapt Content for Different Audiences
   187|
   188|**Audience Adaptation Framework**:
   189|
   190|| Audience | Adjust | Keep | Example |
   191|| --- | --- | --- | --- |
   192|| **From Technical → Business** | Jargon removed, ROI framed, examples simplified | Core insights, data, credibility | Replace "ML algorithms" with "automated decision-making" |
   193|| **From Narrative → Academic** | Anecdotes reduced, formal citations required, neutral tone | Core argument, evidence, originality | Add literature review, formal citations, remove storytelling |
   194|| **From Long-form → Social Media** | Length cut 90%, snappier sentences, hook at start | Main takeaway, credibility, call-to-action | Thread 10 posts from 2000-word article |
   195|| **From Expert → Beginner** | Jargon explained, more examples, foundational context | Nuance, credibility, depth | Add "What is X?" section before diving into analysis |
   196|
   197|**Your Role**: Identify what changes, what stays. Let writer decide adaptation approach.
   198|
   199|---
   200|
   201|**Summary**: These decision trees help you diagnose situations quickly and route to the right approach. When stuck, ask diagnostic questions before offering solutions.
   202|
